title: IRS Code 706 Meaning (Credit Transferred In) – For Tax Professionals
description: IRS Code 706 means a credit was transferred in from another tax period/module. See how it ties to codes 826, 766, 768, and 806, and document it in a client-ready report.
content:
IRS Transcript Codes • Built for 750,000+ U.S. Tax Professionals

IRS Code 706 Meaning

IRS Code 706 means a credit was transferred in from another tax period/module. This page explains how to read it in context, what it usually signals, and how to summarize it cleanly for a client deliverable. Want to see what clients get? Open the sample report, then run your own transcript when you’re ready.

Try the parser (requires credits)

What is IRS Code 706?

Plain-English definition: IRS Code 706 means a credit was transferred in to this tax module from another period/module. It’s not “a refund happened.” It’s “a credit moved.”

Common transcript type
Account Transcript
Usually appears near
Codes 766, 768, 806, 826
Typical context
Offsets, reallocations, credit balancing between periods
Client-friendly summary
"A credit was moved onto this year to cover the balance"

When does Code 706 appear?

  • After an overpayment exists: A credit exists somewhere (another year/module) and gets moved here.
  • During offsets or reallocations: The IRS applies credits to satisfy a balance due in another period/module.
  • During clean-up/corrections: Credit application is corrected or re-applied, which can create “movement” entries.

What does Code 706 usually signal?

If this appears with Code 826

This is the classic “transfer pair” across modules: 826 = transferred out on one module and 706 = transferred in on another. Matching amounts and close dates are the giveaway.

If this appears near Code 806 or 766/768

Those codes can represent common credit sources (withholding and refundable/other credits). Code 706 may show the credit being moved to where it’s needed. The key is whether the transfer reduces a balance due or later contributes to a refund position.

If the client expected a refund

A transfer can explain “my refund is smaller” or “why did my refund disappear.” The credit may have been moved to cover another period/module rather than refunded.

What should a tax professional do next?

  1. Match the transfer: Look for a corresponding transfer-out entry on another module (often 826) with the same amount.
  2. Reconcile the balance: Confirm whether this transfer reduced a balance due, created a zero balance, or still left a balance.
  3. Explain the “why”: Summarize: “A credit from X was moved to Y to cover Z,” using dates/amounts.

Example: how this looks in a parsed report

Below is a simplified view of how Code 706 can appear in a client-ready transcript report with context:

Credit Transfer Timeline (Simplified)
Code 706 – Credit transferred in
Amount: $500 • Date: 10/20/2024
Code 826 – Credit transferred out (on the source module)
Note: Match amount/date across modules to confirm the transfer pair
Code 766 – Credit to your account
Note: Use adjacent credits to narrate the full story

FAQ

What does IRS Code 706 mean on a tax transcript?

IRS Code 706 means a credit was transferred in from another tax period/module. It’s a credit movement entry, so the meaning comes from where it came from and what it was applied against.

Does IRS Code 706 mean my client is getting a refund?

Not necessarily. Code 706 shows a credit transfer in, not a refund release. The transferred credit may reduce a balance due, be offset elsewhere, or later contribute to a refund depending on the remaining module balance.

How does Code 706 relate to Code 826?

A common pairing is Code 826 (credit transferred out) on one module and Code 706 (credit transferred in) on another. Use dates and matching amounts to confirm the transfer and explain the net result to the client.
